steps.title: Decode the researchers' claim
steps.content: The researchers concluded that “correlations with velocity are stronger than those with density.” A stronger correlation means a value further from 0 (regardless of sign). So the correct answer must show that for every rating — Before, After, and Overall — the absolute value of the velocity correlation exceeds the absolute value of the density correlation.

steps.title: Eliminate wrong choices, confirm the match
steps.content:
• Choice A — The correlation between congestion ratings before the obstacle and density is further from 0 than the correlation between overall congestion rating and velocity is.: This compares density (Before) to velocity (Overall) — a cross-category comparison that does not test the researchers’ claim. ✗
  • Choice B — The correlation between congestion ratings before the obstacle and velocity is further from 0 than the correlation between congestion overall and velocity is.: This compares velocity (Before) to velocity (Overall) — two velocity values, not velocity vs. density. ✗
  • Choice C — For each of the three ratings, the correlation with velocity is negative while the correlation with density is positive.: Describes the sign pattern, not relative strength — a negative value close to 0 is weaker than a positive value far from 0. ✗
  • Choice D — For each of the three ratings, correlations with velocity are further from 0 than the corresponding correlations with density are.: Checks all three pairs — Before (|−0.9357| > |0.8592|), After (|−0.9518| > |0.7308|), Overall (|−0.8587| > |0.7447|) — confirming velocity is stronger in every case. ✓
That is the correct answer.
